coredata.dc:title: Synergistically enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of ZnCdS by co-loading graphene quantum dots and PdS dual cocatalysts under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fang
Su, Yanhong
Min, Shixiong
Li, Yanan
Lei, Yonggang
Hou, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report that the co-loading of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and PdS dual cocatalysts on ZnCdS surface achieves a high efficiency photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light (≥420nm). The GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst was prepared by a facile two steps: hydrothermal coupling of GQDs on ZnCdS surface followed by an in-situ chemical deposition of PdS. The resulted GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS exhibits a H2 evolution rate of 517 μmol h−1, which is 15, 7, and 1.7 times higher than that of pure ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, and ZnCdS/PdS, respectively, demonstrating the synergistic effects of GQDs and PdS dual cocatalysts. A high apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) up to 22.4% can be achieved over GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS at 420nm. GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS also has a relatively good stability. Such a considerable enhancement of photocatalytic activity was attributable to the co-loading of the GQDs and PdS as respective reduction and oxidation cocatalysts, leading to an efficient charge separation and surface reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
PdS
ZnCdS
Dual cocatalysts
Photocatalytic H2 evolution
Visible light

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnCdS nanoparticles
Synthesis of GQDs decorated ZnCdS composites (GQDs/ZnCdS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples.
TEM images of (a) ZnCdS, (b) GQDs/ZnCdS, (c) ZnCdS/PdS, and (d) GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples.
High-resolution XPS spectra of Pd 3d in the (a) ZnCdS/PdS and (b) GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore size distribution (inset) of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts. Inset shows the optical images of the samples.

(a) photocatalytic H2 evolution rates over different photocatalysts. (b) AQEs of photocatalytic H2 evolution over ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts under visible light irradiation of 420nm. (c) The stability test for the photocatalytic H2 evolution on GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst. (d) Wavelength-dependent H2 evolution rates on ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts. Conditions: photocatalyst (50mg); 0.5M Na2S and 0.5M Na2SO3 solution (100mL); light source, 300-W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter of 420nm.
Polarization curves of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts in 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S solution.
Steady-state PL spectra of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalysts. Excitation wavelength, 420nm; slit width, 2nm.
Schematic two-step hydrothermal coupling and in-situ chemical deposition of GQDs and PdS on ZnCdS towards preparation of GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism for GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst.
Physicochemical properties of the ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples.

head.ce:title: Synergistically enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of ZnCdS by co-loading graphene quantum dots and PdS dual cocatalysts under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, Ningxia Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application, North Minzu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report that the co-loading of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and PdS dual cocatalysts on ZnCdS surface achieves a high efficiency photocatalytic H2 evolution under visible light (≥420nm). The GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst was prepared by a facile two steps: hydrothermal coupling of GQDs on ZnCdS surface followed by an in-situ chemical deposition of PdS. The resulted GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS exhibits a H2 evolution rate of 517 μmol h−1, which is 15, 7, and 1.7 times higher than that of pure ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, and ZnCdS/PdS, respectively, demonstrating the synergistic effects of GQDs and PdS dual cocatalysts. A high apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) up to 22.4% can be achieved over GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS at 420nm. GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS also has a relatively good stability. Such a considerable enhancement of photocatalytic activity was attributable to the co-loading of the GQDs and PdS as respective reduction and oxidation cocatalysts, leading to an efficient charge separation and surface reactions.

ZnCdS co-loaded with graphene quantum dots and PdS dual cocatalysts showed high activity for H2 evolution with an apparent quantum efficiency of 22.4% at 420nm.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-light-driven photocatalytic H2 generation on semiconductors (SCs) has been widely pursing as a promising strategy to solve environmental issues and to meet increasing energy demands [1–3]. In general, the overall H2 generation efficiency over a photocatalyst is determined by three major factors, light harvesting efficiency, charge separation/transport efficiency, and surface chemical reaction efficiency [4–7]. Apart from developing highly stable and visible-light-responsive photocatalysts [7], it has been widely recognized that the loading of a reduction cocatalyst, an oxidation cocatalyst, or dual cocatalysts (both reduction and oxidation cocatalysts) is effective in improving charge separation/transport and surface reaction efficiencies [8], as a result of enhancing solar to H2 conversion efficiency. Compared to the deposition of either a reduction cocatalyst or oxidation cocatalyst that can only trap photogenerated electrons or holes, the loading of both reduction and oxidation cocatalysts onto the same photocatalyst would be expected to realize spatial separation of photogenerated charge carriers so that more electrons and holes can be utilized for carrying out the reduction and oxidation reaction, respectively [8]. In this context, Li et al. [9,10] reported that CdS co-loaded with Pt and PdS (Pt-PdS/CdS) leads to an extremely high quantum efficiency of 93% for photocatalytic H2 production under visible light irradiation. Following this pioneer work, several combinations of reduction and oxidation cocatalysts was implemented to improve the photocatalytic H2 generation efficiency of SC photocatalysts (Pt-RuO2/Zn2GeO4 [11], Rh/Cr2O3-Mn3O4/GaN:ZnO [12], Pt-Co3O4/TiO2 [13], Pd-IrOx/TiO2 [14], Pt-RuO2/TiO2 [15], Pt-CoPi/Bi0.5Y0.5VO4 [16], Pt-MnOx/BiVO4 [17], Pt-Co3O4/SrTiO3 [18], NiS-PdS/CdS [19], Ti(IV)−Ni(II)/CdS [20]). However, the use of high-price and scarce noble metals as reduction or oxidation cocatalyst significantly limits their applications in scale-up photocatalytic H2 production. Therefore, the development of highly efficient reduction or oxidation cocatalyst based on earth-abundant elements is highly desired but remains challenging.

Recently, numerous noble-metal-free and low-cost materials including transition metal oxides/hydroxides [21] (e.g. NiO [22], CoOx [23], MnOx [12,17], Ni(OH)2 [24], Cu(OH)2 [25]), metal sulfides (e.g. MoS2 [26–29], WS2 [30–32], CoS [33], NiS [34], CuSx [35]), and carbon nanomaterials (carbon black [36], fullerene [37], carbon nanotubes [38], graphene [39,40], graphene quantum dots [41–49]) have been found to be capable of replacing noble metal cocatalysts to promote the photocatalytic H2 production activity. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that possesses high electron reservation capability, tunable bandgap, strong visible light absorption, and excellent photostability [50–54], has been widely used to modify the SCs in order to develop advanced nanohybrids with enhanced performances [43–47]. In the previous studies, it is reported that loading of GQDs on TiO2 [43–45], CdSe [46], and CdS [47] can greatly promote the photocatalytic efficiency of resulting composites for H2 evolution reaction, where the GQDs can act as an efficient electron acceptor to induce an efficient charge separation and act as an active sites for facilitating the H2 evolution reaction. Although the role of GQDs as a H2 evolution cocatalyst have been demonstrated and enhanced activity on the GQDs loaded SCs (GQDs/SCs) photocatalysts has been achieved, the photocatalytic efficiency of GQDs/SCs photocatalysts is far lower than that on the noble metal loaded one [44–47], and need to be further improved. It has been turned out that suitable loading of dual cocatalysts would improve the photocatalytic activity of a photocatalyst, thus it would be rational to expect that loading an oxidation cocatalyst onto the GQDs/SCs photocatalyst would result in a synergistic effect to improve water reduction to form H2 gas.

Herein, in order to further improve the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs loaded ZnCdS (GQDs/ZnCdS) photocatalyst, low-crystalline PdS as an oxidation cocatalyst were facilely co-loaded onto the GQDs/ZnCdS surface to form GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst by a simple chemical deposition method. It is found that the co-loading of GQDs and PdS leads to an obvious synergistic effect and the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst can achieve a high H2 evolution rate of 517 μmol h−1 and an apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 22.4% at 420nm for photocatalytic H2 evolution in the presence of 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S aqueous solution as sacrificial reagents. Furthermore, the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst shows a rather long-term stability for H2 evolution within 24h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and ZnCdS nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: All the reagents were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to our previously reported procedure [47]. The ZnCdS nanoparticles with a nominal Zn:Cd ratio of 0.5:0.5 were prepared as follows: an aqueous solution of Na2S (1000mL, 0.27M) was added slowly to a mixed solution (800mL) containing Zn(NO3)2 (0.14M) and Cd(NO3)2 (0.14M) under vigorous stirring. The yellow mixture was stirred for 48h. The resulting yellow slurry was filtered, thoroughly washed with water, and dried at 60 ◦C for 24h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs decorated ZnCdS composites (GQDs/ZnCdS)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs decorated ZnCdS composites (GQDs/ZnCdS) composites were prepared by a facile hydrothermal deposition method. Typically, 0.8g of ZnCdS nanoparticles was dispersed into 60mL of DI water containing GQDs with ultrasonication and stirring for 0.5h at room temperature to form a homogeneous suspension. The weight percentage of GQDs in GQDs/ZnCdS was 1.0wt%. Next, the suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100mL) and maintained at 180°C for 12h. After cooled to room temperature, the product was collected by filtration through a 0.22µm microporous membrane, washed with water and ethanol for several times, and dried in air overnight. For comparison, pure ZnCdS was also treated under the exact same hydrothermal conditions used for the preparation of GQDs/ZnCdS.

The loading of PdS on pure ZnCdS or GQDs/ZnCdS producing ZnCdS/PdS or GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS was carried out by a in-situ chemical deposition process. A Pd(OAc)2 solution (2.4mg/mL) was added dropwise to a suspension of ZnCdS or GQDs/ZnCdS powder (0.05g, as prepared above) dispersed in 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S aqueous solution and stirred for 0.5h prior to the photocatalytic reaction. The nominal loading amount of PdS was controlled to be 0.5wt%. After the completion of photocatalytic reaction, the products were collected from reaction suspension by centrifugation, rinsed with distilled water and ethanol each for three times, and dried in air overnight for characterization purpose.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with a Tecnai-G2-F30 field emission transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were investigated with a Rigaku B/Max-RB diffractometer with a nickel filtrated Cu Kɑ radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of the samples were performed on a K-Alpha surface analysis (Thermon Scientific) using X-ray monochromatization. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis–DRS) were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis–near-IR spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere and BaSO4 powders were used as a reflectance standard. The specific surface area was determined with the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation at 77K by using an adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics ASAP 2020 HD88). Photoluminescence spectra were determined by a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer.

The photocatalytic hydrogen production experiments were performed in a sealed reactor with a top flat quartz window for light irradiation and a silicone rubber septum was fixed on its side for sampling produced H2 in the headspace of reaction cell. A 300-W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300) equipped with an optical cut-off filter (λ≥420nm) was used as light source and the effective irradiation area was determined to be 28.9cm2. In a typical experiment, 50.0mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed into 100mL of 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S aqueous solution by ultrasonication for 5min. Before irradiation, the reaction solution was thoroughly degassed by repeated evacuation-N2 filling, and finally refilled with N2 to reach ambient pressure. The reaction solution was continuously stirred and cooled by a flow of cooling water. During the irradiation, the amount of evolved H2 was analyzed with a gas chromatography (Tech comp; GC-7890II) with a thermal conductivity detector, a 5A molecular sieve column, and with N2 as carrying gas. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was measured under the same photocatalytic reaction conditions with irradiation light through a band-pass filter of 420nm. The photon flux of incident light was determined using a Ray virtual radiation actinometer (Apogee MQ-500, silicon ray detector, light spectrum, 389–692nm; measurement range, 0–4000 μmolm−2 s−1) and the AQE was calculated according to the following equation:
AQE [ % ] =
numberofreactedelectrons
numberofincidentphotons
× 100
=
2 ×
numberofevolvedH
2
molecules
numberofincidentphotons
× 100

Electrochemical measurements were conducted with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China) in a home-made three-electrode quartz cell using Pt sheet as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared on clean and conductive side of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass (ca. 1.0cm × 1.5cm) by drop-casting 0.25mL of the sample slurry, which was obtained by dispersing 4mg sample in 1mL of H2O with the assistance of sonication. After air-drying, the working electrode was further dried at 200°C for 0.5h under vacuum conditions to improve adhesion. The effective irradiation areas of the electrodes were controlled to be 1.0×1.0cm2. To ensure a certain stability of the electrodes, a mixed solution containing 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S was used as supporting electrolyte and sacrificial reagent with N2 bubbling (40mLmin−1) for 10min before tests.

The preparation of GQDs and PdS co-loaded ZnCdS (GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS) consists of two steps: hydrothermal coupling of GQDs on ZnCdS surface followed by facile in-situ chemical deposition of PdS, as shown in Scheme 1. The as-prepared GQDs were highly dispersible in solution with an average lateral size of 4.3nm (Fig. S1) and showed highly fluorescent with an excitation wavelength-independent emission feature [47]. The GQDs/ZnCdS was prepared by a facile hydrothermal method from a suspension containing as-prepared GQDs and ZnCdS. Pure ZnCdS were prepared under the same experimental conditions except that the GQD was absent. Afterwards, PdS was loaded on the ZnCdS or GQDs/ZnCdS by adding Pd salt into the 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S aqueous solution prior to photocatalytic reaction [9,10], leading to the formation of ZnCdS/PdS or GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS.

Fig. 1 presents the XRD patterns of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples. Notes that all diffraction peaks for above four samples shift towards lower-angle compared to the cubic ZnS and higher-angle with respective to cubic CdS, indicating that the ZnCdS in all samples is a solid solution rather than just a physical mixture of ZnS and CdS. There are three characteristic peaks at 2θ value of 27.4, 45.4, and 53.5°, respectively, which can be indexed to the (111), (220), and (311) crystal planes of cubic ZnCdS, consistent with the observations in literatures [55]. The GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples exhibit similar diffraction peak position and intensity to pure ZnCdS, implying that loading of GQDs, PdS, or GQDs and PdS does not have a significant influence on the phase structure and crystallinity of ZnCdS [47]. Furthermore, no characteristic diffraction peaks for GQDs or PdS can be observed on the XRD patterns for these samples probably due to their low loading amount and relatively low diffraction intensity [43,44]. Fig. 2a exhibits the TEM image of the pure ZnCdS, revealing that the pure ZnCdS nanoparticles are less aggregated with a size of 30–50nm. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the GQDs/ZnCdS sample, in which a thin GQDs layer could be clearly observed on the surfaces of well-crystallized ZnCdS nanoparticles, forming a strongly-coupled GQDs/ZnCdS photocatalyst. The intimate contact between GQDs and ZnCdS would be in favor of efficient charge transfer across the GQDs/ZnCdS interface and consequently reduce the recombination of photogenerated carries [47]. In HRTEM image of ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst (Fig. 2c), low-crystalline PdS and/or Pd0 species could be observed, indicating the success in-situ loading of PdS and/or Pd0 on ZnCdS nanoparticles. The HRTEM image of GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS in Fig. 2d reveals that GQDs and PdS are spatially deposited on the different surface sites of ZnCdS nanoparticles. Furthermore, it can be seen that the GQDs and PdS are in close contact with the ZnCdS. As reported previously, GQDs can be viewed as a H2 evolution sites when combined with semiconductor photocatalysts, while PdS can act as an oxidation sites to promote the charge separation. Thus, for GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst, the spatial separation of GQDs and PdS would be in favor of the maximum separation of photogenerated charge carriers, as a result, an enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution can be expected on ZnCdS when co-loaded with GQDs and PdS dual cocatalysts.

The chemical compositions and the chemical states of Pd on photocatalyst samples were further studied by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS). The results show that the Zn, Cd, S, C, and O elements can be found for all the samples, while the Pd signal can only be detected from ZnCdS/PdS and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples (Fig. S2). High-resolution XPS spectrum of Pd 3d from the ZnCdS/PdS sample shows that the dominant valence for the Pd species on ZnCdS is PdII, which is consistent with PdS, while a small portion of Pd°, about 1/11 of that PdII in PdS ( Fig. 3a) [9], could also be observed. For Pd 3d signal from GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS sample, there is only PdII in PdS, no metallic Pd° could be observed (Fig. 3b). In addition, the binding energy of Pd 3d for GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS is higher than that of Pd 3d for ZnCdS/PdS sample by 0.37eV, which is not only indicate the directional electron transfer from the loaded PdS to the ZnCdS and to GQDs [9,10], but also indicate strong interaction and intimate contact between these three components.

As shown in Fig. 4, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples can be classified into type IV isotherm with type H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of mesopores. In addition, all the isotherms show a rapidly increased adsorption at a relative pressure (P/P 0) ranging from 0.8 to 1.0, also implying the presence of macropores. The coexistence of mesopores and macropores in the samples is further confirmed by their pore size distribution curves in inset of Fig. 4, where the pore size distributed widely from 2 to 100nm with two peaks centered at ca. 3 and 18nm, respectively. The formation of mesopores probably is a result of the aggregation of primary ZnCdS crystallites of 10–15nm, while the macrospores may come from the aggregation of larger ZnCdS particles [45]. Table 1 compares the Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET) surface areas (S BET), average pore size, and total pore volume for above four samples. Clearly, loading of GQDs and PdS largely increase the S BET of pure ZnCdS from 40.7 to 90.9 and 74.4m2 g−1 for the GQDs/ZnCdS and the ZnCdS/PdS samples, respectively. As expected, GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS sample shows the largest S BET of 105.7m2 g−1 due to the co-loading of GQDs and PdS. In addition, it is found that the average pore size of the samples exhibits a reversed variation trend to S BET, while their total pore volumes are nearly the same [45,47]. These results suggest that the loading of GQDs or PdS may partially block the mesopores and macropores but their existence may act as a surface spacer to prevent the ZnCdS nanoparticles from aggregations as reflected by the increased S BET. The enhanced S BET would be beneficial to provide more active sites for the H2 evolution reactions.

The optical features of the ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples were studied by the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra ( Fig. 5). All the samples exhibit intense absorption at wavelength shorter than 500nm, corresponding to the intrinsic band-gap absorption of ZnCdS. The estimated band-gap energies are 2.45, 2.44, 2.42, and 2.41eV for ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples, respectively (Fig. S3). This result reveals that the surface coupling of GQDs has no significant effect on the intrinsic optical properties of ZnCdS and the GQDs most likely exist on the surface of ZnCdS nanoparticles instead of doping into the ZnCdS lattices [46]. For PdS loaded samples, a band-gap narrowing could be observed probably due to the formation of heterojunction at the contact interface of ZnCdS and PdS [9,10]. Additionally, an enhanced light absorption is observed from 500 to 800nm for the GQDs/ZnCdS and ZnCdS/PdS samples, accompanying with the color deepening due to the adsorption of GQDs and PdS. As to the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS sample, a pronounced enhancement for visible-light absorption is observed due to the coexistence of GQDs and PdS. Although the loading of GQDs and PdS could induce an extra visible light absorption at longer wavelengths, whether or not these absorbed photons can effectively excite photocatalysts to generate electrons and holes that participate in photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction is still uncertain and need to be clarified further (see detailed discussion below).

Fig. 6a shows the photocatalytic activities for H2 evolution over different samples. As expected, pure ZnCdS without the cocatalysts loaded shows very low activity for H2 evolution (ca. 35 μmol h−1). The H2 evolution activity is doubled (74 μmol h−1) when 1wt% GQDs was loaded on ZnCdS. When 0.5wt% PdS was in-situ loaded as the cocatalyst, the ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst shows a remarkably enhanced H2 evolution rate of 301 μmol h−1, which is 8.6 and 4.1 times higher activity than that of pure ZnCdS and GQDs/ZnCdS, respectively. Notably, the activity of GQDs/ZnCdS is even enhanced further by a factor of 1.7 when PdS is co-loaded, and the H2 evolution rate of GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS is estimated to be as high as 517 μmol h−1, corresponding to an AQE of 22.4% at 420nm (Fig. 6b). Note that the physical mixtures of GQDs+ZnCdS and GQDs+ZnCdS/PdS exhibit inferior photocatalytic activities as compared to ZnCdS/GQDs and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS, indicating that the effective coupling of ZnCdS and GQDs is vital to obtain enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity [47]. In addition to its high H2 evolution activity, the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst is relatively stable towards long-time photocatalytic H2 evolution, as shown in Fig. 6c. Within the prolonged visible light irradiation of 24h, the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst gradually deactivated with the reaction time, and there is a 28% loss in H2 evolution activity after 24h reaction. The decrease in activity could be largely attributed to the photocorrosion of ZnCdS even with the loading of cocatalysts [29].

Above results clearly reveal that co-loading of GQDs and PdS on ZnCdS leads to an obvious synergistic effect to enhance the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of ZnCdS, thus the essential roles of GQDs and PdS need to be clarified. On the basis of UV–vis–DRS spectra, it was observed that the loading of GQDs, PdS, or co-loading of GQDs and PdS on ZnCdS can induce an extra light absorption in the longer wavelengths of visible light region to certain extent, it could be expected that the loaded GQDs and PdS on ZnCdS surface may have a capability of photosensitizer to harness more light photons during the H2 evolution reactions. To verify this hypothesis, we performed incident light wavelength-dependent H2 evolution experiments (Fig. 6d). It is found that all these photocatalysts can catalyze H2 evolution under the light irradiation shorter than 420 and 470nm. As the cut-off wavelength of incident light approaches to the absorption edge of the ZnCdS (~550nm), the H2 evolution activity decreases sharply at cut-off wavelength of 520nm, and only trace H2 is evolved for all the photocatalysts as the wavelength beyond 550nm. This result indicates that the H2 evolution rate over these photocatalyst corresponds well to the optical absorption of ZnCdS, and the excess light absorption coming from the (co) loading of GQDs, PdS, or GQDs and PdS hardly contribute to the observed high activity for cocatalyst-loaded ZnCdS. This result also suggests that the loaded GQDs and PdS mainly play a cocatalyst role to induce surface redox reactions rather than a photosensitizer for GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst [47].

In order to further reveal the cocatalyst roles of GQDs and PdS, the electrochemical behavior of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS thin films coated on FTO conductive glasses was examined. Fig. 7 shows the polarization curves of these thin films in 0.5M Na2SO3 and 0.5M Na2S aqueous solution in the potential range from +0.5 to −1.5V vs. SCE. When the electrodes were polarized to cathodic direction, the current densities are increased, consistent with their photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. Due to the absence of cocatalyst, the ZnCdS/FTO electrode exhibits the lowest cathodic current density, accompany with a low H2 evolution activity. After GQDs or PdS being loaded on ZnCdS, an increased cathodic current density could be observed for GQDs/ZnCdS/FTO and ZnCdS/PdS/FTO. This result reveals that the GQDs in GQDs/ZnCdS can serve as a reduction cocatalyst to catalyze proton reduction to form H2, consistent with our previous results [45,47], while the small portion of Pd° existed in ZnCdS/PdS can also efficiently catalyze the H2 evolution [9,10]. A further enhancement of cathodic current density is then observed for GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS/FTO electrode, suggesting that co-loading of GQDs and PdS can synergistically promote the reduction reaction. On the other hand, when the electrodes were polarized to more positive potentials, the anodic current densities attributed to the oxidation of electron donor are increased with the variation trend similar to their cathodic current densities. This result suggests the loading of PdS will greatly facilitate the electron transfer from PdS to ZnCdS across the semiconductor-electrolyte interface to oxidizing the sacrificial reagents S2- and/or SO3 2-, consistent with the previous reports [10], showing that PdS acts as an oxidation cocatalyst.

Since GQDs and PdS that act as respective reduction and oxidation cocatalysts were co-loaded on ZnCdS, the photoexcited electron transfer and recombination processes were investigated to further shed light on the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity over GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst. Fig. 8 shows steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnCdS, GQDs/ZnCdS, ZnCdS/PdS, and GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS samples. The dominant PL emission of pure ZnCdS is located at around 530nm, which comes from the band-to-band emission of ZnCdS. Compared to ZnCdS, the PL emission intensities of the GQDs/ZnCdS and ZnCdS/PdS decreased significantly, indicating that the loadings of GQDs or PdS could greatly facilitate the charge transfer and separation [44–47]. Notably, the GQDs and PdS co-loaded ZnCdS exhibits the lowest PL emission intensity, suggesting that the charge separation on GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS is more efficient than that of ZnCdS only loaded with GQDs or PdS. The enhanced charge separation efficiency will lead to an improved photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution reaction. Based on aforementioned results, a plausible mechanism to explain the important roles of co-loading of GQDs and PdS for enhancing the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity over ZnCdS were proposed, as shown in Scheme 2. Under the visible light irradiation, the pure ZnCdS results in a very low activity due to the fast charge recombination. In strong contrast, the GQDs/ZnCdS exhibits higher activity than pure ZnCdS because the GQDs can act as an electron acceptor to efficiently capture the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band (CB) of ZnCdS, leading to an efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus more electrons can participate in the H2 evolution reaction. On the other hand, when PdS was loaded on ZnCdS, the resulting ZnCdS/PdS shows much higher activity than pure ZnCdS and GQDs/ZnCdS, where PdS could not only act as a hole acceptor for enhancing charge separation efficiency [9,10,19], but also a oxidation cocatalyst to accelerate the oxidation half reaction of sacrificial agents (S2- and SO3 2-) to its degraded products (S2O3 2- and SO4 2-) [47,56], thereby promoting the reduction half reaction of H+ to form H2. After spatial co-loading of GQDs acting as an reduction cocatalyst and PdS acting as an oxidation cocatalyst, a more efficient separation and transfer of the photogenerated electrons and holes and an optimized surface reaction kinetics could be achieved, thus the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS exhibits the extremely high activity for H2 evolution under visible light irradiation.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated that co-loading of GQDs and PdS as respective reduction and oxidation cocatalyst is an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic H2 evolution efficiency of ZnCdS photocatalyst. Under visible light irradiation, the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst exhibits a high H2 evolution rate of 517 μmol h−1, which is much higher than ZnCdS loading with GQDs or PdS single cocatalyst by 7 and 1.7 times, respectively. An AQE of 22.4% for H2 evolution was also achieved at 420nm. The enhancement in photocatalytic H2 evolution activity over the GQDs/ZnCdS/PdS photocatalyst can be attributed to the synergetic effect of co-loaded dual cocatalysts, where GQDs act as a reduction cocatalyst and PdS oxidation cocatalyst, respectively, which can greatly prohibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and provide catalytic active sites for photocatalytic reduction and oxidation reactions, as a result of improving photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of ZnCdS. This work not only shows the possibility of GQDs as an efficient reduction cocatalyst in replace of noble metals, but also provides a new route for the development of highly efficient photocatalysts with dual cocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Key Scientific Research Projects of the Higher Education Institutions of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (Grant no. NCX2017143).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jssc.2018.01.010.
